tracker_summary.invention: The Internet (packet-switched global computer network) and the World Wide Web
tracker_summary.inventor: No single inventor — key figures include Paul Baran, Donald Davies, Lawrence Roberts, Vint Cerf, Bob Kahn, and Tim Berners-Lee
tracker_summary.date: 1969 (ARPANET first link); 1983 (TCP/IP switch-over); 1991 (World Wide Web public release)
tracker_summary.current_use: Global infrastructure connecting ~5.4 billion people, underpinning commerce, communication, science, and culture
tracker_summary.initial_funding_source: U.S. Department of Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency (ARPA)
tracker_summary.initial_funding_reason: Fear that a Soviet nuclear first strike could destroy centralized U.S. command-and-control communications

the_x: ARPA funding to survive nuclear decapitation of U.S. command communications
the_y: The World Wide Web and the modern Internet used by over half of humanity
summary: In the late 1950s, U.S. military planners were terrified that a Soviet ICBM strike could sever all command-and-control communications in a single blow. ARPA funded research into a network with no center. That idea — packet switching — was invented independently by Paul Baran at RAND and Donald Davies at NPL, became ARPANET, evolved into the Internet through TCP/IP, and was transformed by Tim Berners-Lee's World Wide Web — originally a document management hack at a physics lab. A weapon against nuclear annihilation became the connective tissue of civilization.

chain.what_they_were_doing: ARPA founded after Sputnik; Baran proposed chopping messages into addressed blocks through a distributed mesh to survive nuclear attack.
chain.the_jump: AT&T declared it 'impossible and unnecessary.' Their president told Baran it couldn't work and they didn't want it. The project was shelved.

chain.who: Donald Davies (NPL); Lawrence Roberts (ARPA); Frank Heart (BBN)
chain.where: Teddington UK; Washington D.C.; Cambridge MA
chain.what_they_were_doing: Davies coined 'packet switching' independently; Roberts synthesized both approaches into ARPANET's design; BBN delivered IMP #1 to UCLA August 29, 1969.
chain.the_jump: UK government declined to fund Davies's national network — the architecture migrated to America, where ARPA had money and urgency.

chain.who: Charley Kline, Leonard Kleinrock; Bill Duvall at SRI
chain.where: UCLA Boelter Hall → Stanford Research Institute
chain.what_they_were_doing: Kline typed 'LOGIN'; the system crashed after two letters. The first message in Internet history was 'LO.'
Combined a mailbox program with file transfer, chose the @ symbol, sent the first email — then told a colleague not to mention it because ARPA hadn't paid for it.
chain.the_jump: By 1972, email was 75% of all network traffic. Nobody predicted it. An unsanctioned side project became the first killer app.

chain.who: Vint Cerf, Bob Kahn; Jon Postel
chain.what_they_were_doing: Cerf and Kahn designed TCP/IP to connect incompatible networks; January 1 1983 'Flag Day' forced mandatory cutover from NCP.
chain.the_jump: The OSI model, backed by ISO, ITU, and every European government, was the official rival standard. TCP/IP won because it was free, open, and already running.

chain.what_they_were_doing: NSFNet became the backbone after ARPANET was decommissioned; Berners-Lee submitted a proposal his supervisor annotated 'Vague but exciting' and built HTML, HTTP, URLs, and the first web server.
chain.the_jump: Gopher was more popular in 1992–93, then the University of Minnesota announced licensing fees. The Web was free. Gopher collapsed overnight.

chain.who: Marc Andreessen, Eric Bina (NCSA); Jim Clark; NSF
chain.where: University of Illinois; Mountain View CA
chain.what_they_were_doing: Andreessen built Mosaic on $6.85/hour, driving 341,634% Web traffic growth; co-founded Netscape, which IPO'd with stock hitting $75 on day one; NSF lifted the Acceptable Use Policy the same year.
chain.the_jump: Microsoft had missed the Internet entirely. Gates's 'Internet Tidal Wave' memo reversed course. The commercial Internet era began.

sources: Cerf & Kahn, 'A Protocol for Packet Network Intercommunication,' IEEE, 1974. https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/1092259
First ARPANET IMP log, UCLA, Oct 29, 1969. https://www.computerhistory.org/revolution/networking/19/376/1956
Internet Hall of Fame — Ray Tomlinson. https://internethalloffame.org/inductees/ray-tomlinson
Internet Hall of Fame — Vint Cerf. https://internethalloffame.org/inductees/vint-cerf
CERN statement releasing WWW into public domain, April 30, 1993. https://tenyears-www.web.cern.ch/tenyears-www/Welcome.html
Hafner & Lyon, 'Where Wizards Stay Up Late.' Simon & Schuster, 1996.
